title: Company Secretary Services
content: **[COMPANY SECRETARY SERVICES (PRIVATE COMPANIES)]{.underline}**
**A. Service Overview**

Private companies in South Africa aren't legally required to appoint company secretaries---but that doesn't mean you don't need one . Governance doesn't disappear because you're a (Pty) Ltd. CIPC filings still have deadlines, board resolutions still require proper documentation, statutory registers must be maintained, and shareholders still need transparency . Company secretary services give private companies professional governance support without the cost of a full-time appointment .
**B. Detailed Description**

Company secretaries ensure companies comply with the Companies Act, maintain proper records, support boards with governance, and act as the communication link between directors and shareholders . While the Companies Act only mandates company secretaries for public and state-owned companies, private companies still face the same governance obligations---they just don't need a designated person called "company secretary" .
Why private companies benefit from company secretary services:

🚨 Governance obligations don't disappear because you're private -- Private companies must still maintain statutory registers, file annual returns with CIPC, record board resolutions, keep shareholder records, and comply with the Companies Act . Someone has to do this work---and doing it wrong has consequences .

🚨 CIPC deadlines don't wait---and penalties add up fast -- Annual returns, director changes, share transfers, MOI amendments---all require CIPC filings within strict deadlines. Late filings result in penalties, deregistration threats, and compliance complications .

🚨 Board resolutions need legal precision -- Appointing directors, issuing shares, approving financial statements, declaring dividends, entering major contracts---all require properly drafted board resolutions . Informal decisions or incomplete resolutions create legal risks when disputes arise or transactions are challenged .

🚨 Investors and lenders demand proper governance -- Private equity investors, venture capitalists, banks, and business partners expect professional governance. Poor record-keeping, missing resolutions, or non-compliant processes undermine credibility and deal flow .

🚨 Statutory registers aren't optional -- The Companies Act requires companies to maintain registers of directors, shareholders, company secretaries (if appointed), beneficial owners, and company securities . Inspectors, auditors, shareholders, and courts can demand access---and missing or incorrect registers create liability .

🚨 Directors need independent advice---not just CFO opinions -- Company secretaries provide independent governance advice ensuring directors understand their fiduciary duties, conflicts of interest, and legal obligations under the Companies Act . This independence protects directors from inadvertent breaches .
🚨 You don't need a full-time employee -- Outsourced company secretary services provide professional governance support at a fraction of the cost of hiring someone full-time .

**E. Case Study or Testimonial**
The Company That Nearly Lost a R5 Million Investment---Because of Missing Board Resolutions
A tech startup in Gauteng with three directors and five shareholders raised seed funding and was ready for Series A. A private equity firm offered R5 million for a 25% stake. Due diligence began .
The investor's lawyers requested:
1\. Board resolutions for all major decisions over the past three years
2\. Shareholder register
3\. CIPC compliance certificate
4\. Director appointment resolutions
5\. Share allotment documentation
The startup had none of it.
❌ Board decisions were made via WhatsApp---no formal resolutions
❌ The shareholder register was an outdated Excel sheet
❌ CIPC annual returns were two years overdue
❌ One director had never been formally appointed---just "joined the team"
❌ Shares issued to early employees had no written resolutions or transfer documentation
The investor's response: "Fix your governance or we walk. You have 30 days" .
They called OptiHR in panic. We conducted an emergency company secretarial intervention:
✔ Reconstructed missing board resolutions -- Drafted retrospective resolutions (where legally permissible) for all major decisions, securing director signatures
✔ Formalized director appointment -- Proper board resolution appointing the third director retroactively
✔ Updated shareholder register -- Created compliant register with all historical share movements documented
✔ Filed overdue CIPC returns -- Submitted two years of annual returns with penalty payments
✔ Documented share allotments -- Created proper share certificates and transfer documentation
✔ Established ongoing governance processes -- Board meeting schedules, resolution templates, and compliance calendar
Twenty-eight days later, the due diligence package was complete. The investor approved. The R5 million investment closed .
Twelve months later (with OptiHR as ongoing company secretary):
🔹 100% CIPC compliance---annual returns filed on time
🔹 All board decisions properly documented with resolutions
🔹 Statutory registers maintained accurately
🔹 Second investment round closed smoothly---due diligence found zero governance issues
🔹 Directors receive independent governance advice protecting them from fiduciary breaches

"We almost lost R5 million because we didn't know governance matters for private companies. OptiHR rescued us, then showed us how professional governance opens doors instead of closing them. We're never running a company without proper secretarial support again." -- CEO, Tech Startup, Gauteng
**F. Compliance & Legal Considerations**
Company secretary services ensure compliance with South African corporate law :
📌 Companies Act 71 of 2008 -- Governs company formation, governance, directors' duties, shareholder rights, and compliance obligations .
📌 Section 86: Company Secretary Appointment -- Mandatory for public and state-owned companies; optional for private companies unless MOI requires it .
📌 Section 24: Statutory Registers -- All companies must maintain prescribed registers of directors, shareholders, securities, and beneficial owners .
📌 Section 33: Annual Returns -- Every company must submit annual returns to CIPC. Late submissions result in penalties and potential deregistration .
📌 Sections 61-65: Board Meetings and Resolutions -- Board decisions must follow prescribed procedures. Written resolutions require unanimous director consent .
📌 Sections 59-60: Shareholders' Meetings -- AGMs and special meetings require proper notice, quorum, and voting procedures .
📌 Section 76: Directors' Fiduciary Duties -- Directors must act in good faith, for proper purpose, and in the company's best interests. Company secretaries advise on these duties .
📌 Section 75: Personal Liability -- Directors can be held personally liable for breaches of duty. Proper governance reduces this risk .
📌 King IV Corporate Governance Code -- While not legally binding for private companies, King IV principles represent governance best practices .
📌 MOI Amendments (Section 16) -- Require special resolutions and CIPC filings .

**H. FAQ (1--3 for each service)**
Q1: If private companies don't legally need company secretaries, why appoint one?

Because governance obligations don't disappear . You still must maintain statutory registers, file CIPC returns, record board decisions properly, and comply with the Companies Act . Someone has to do this work---and doing it wrong creates legal risks, investor credibility issues, and compliance penalties . Outsourced company secretary services give you professional governance without hiring full-time employees .
Q2: What happens if we don't file CIPC annual returns on time?

CIPC imposes penalties for late filings---typically R50-R100 per month overdue . More seriously, prolonged non-compliance can result in deregistration threats, inability to conduct business legally, and complications when seeking contracts, loans, or investments . Courts, investors, and business partners expect current CIPC compliance---non-compliance signals poor governance .
Q3: Can our CFO or financial manager just handle company secretarial work?

They can try---but company secretary work requires corporate law knowledge, Companies Act expertise, and governance understanding that most finance professionals don't have . More importantly, company secretaries should provide independent advice to boards, which internal staff can't do objectively . Outsourcing to professionals ensures legal precision, independence, and compliance .
